findings.title: Race condition: shared issues array mutated concurrently inside Promise.all
findings.description: verifyDistribution builds a single `issues` array and hands closures over it to Promise.all. Each async branch calls `issues.push(...ytIssues)` concurrently. JavaScript's Array.push is not atomic across await boundaries, but since each push resolves after its own await the mutations are interleaved: the reads and writes to the shared array happen on the same micro-task turn after each awaited fetch resolves, so there is no data-loss in V8's single-threaded JS model here for push alone. However, the real hazard is correctness of the final result: if any platform's try/catch swallows its error and pushes an api_check issue, while another platform pushes title issues at the same moment, the ordering in the final issues array is non-deterministic. This is low-severity for correctness but documents the intent mismatch.
findings.impact: Non-deterministic issue ordering in the verification report and email; no data is lost but the notification may be confusing.
findings.recommendation: Collect results from each branch as return values of the mapped promises and flatten after await Promise.all, rather than closing over a shared mutable array.

findings.title: YouTube title comparison in verifyYouTube does not HTML-decode — false verification failures for titles with special characters
findings.description: verifyYouTube compares `video.snippet.title` (returned raw from the YouTube Data API v3) against `expectedTitle` (the plain-text string stored in the job). The YouTube API returns snippet.title already decoded — e.g. '&' appears as '&' — so no decoding is needed there. However verifyWordPress on line 308 does decode HTML entities before comparing. The inconsistency is not a bug on its own, but if the YouTube API ever returns entity-encoded text (which some older API versions did), the comparison would produce false positives. More concretely: the WordPress verifier decodes entities; the YouTube verifier does not. If the title contains an apostrophe stored by WordPress as ’ but returned by YouTube as a literal curly-apostrophe, the comparison fails and fires a spurious notification.
findings.impact: False-positive verification failure emails for episode titles containing apostrophes, ampersands, or non-ASCII characters.
findings.recommendation: Apply the same HTML-entity decode logic in verifyYouTube that verifyWordPress already uses, or extract it to a shared helper.

findings.title: Live-recording path: processor skips audio extraction but only when gcsPath is absent — if job has a pre-existing gcsPath (retry), audio is extracted from video rather than YouTube download
findings.description: The guard at line 148 is `if (existingYoutubeUrl && !effectiveGcsPath)`. On the first run this is correct: no gcsPath exists yet, so YouTube audio is downloaded. But if the job is retried after a partial failure (e.g. Transistor upload failed but the YouTube download succeeded and gcsPath was persisted on line 153-154), `effectiveGcsPath` will be non-null on retry. The condition `!effectiveGcsPath` is false, so `youtubeAudioPath` stays null. Then at line 171 `gcsAudioPath = youtubeAudioPath` is null, `transistorNeedsWork` is true, `effectiveGcsPath` is the previously-stored mp3 path, and `!gcsAudioPath` is true — so `extractAudio()` is called on the GCS mp3 path (the audio-only file), which is likely to succeed but wastes resources and re-extracts audio from audio. This is functionally harmless but wasteful. Worse: if `extractAudio` expects a video container and gets an mp3, it may fail, blocking the retry.
findings.impact: On retry of a live-recording job, `extractAudio` may be called on the already-downloaded mp3 file instead of skipping the step, potentially failing the retry.
findings.recommendation: Track the youtube-audio-downloaded state in job metadata (e.g. `youtubeAudioDownloaded: true`) so the retry path also sets `youtubeAudioPath` and skips extractAudio.

findings.title: updateDistribution double-applies toISOWithTimezone: client may pass an already-converted ISO string
findings.description: In the AI path, distributeAfterAi (distribution-form.tsx line 465) passes `publishState.date` directly as `scheduledAt` — this is a string from the PublishToggle component, which is a naive datetime string like `2026-05-01T09:00`. updateDistribution then calls `toISOWithTimezone(data.scheduledAt, data.timezone)` at line 246. This is correct. However, toISOWithTimezone at timezone.ts:21 does `new Date(naiveDatetime.endsWith('Z') ? naiveDatetime : naiveDatetime + 'Z')` to determine DST. This means `2026-05-01T09:00` becomes `2026-05-01T09:00Z` (UTC), which is used only to resolve the offset — the base string itself is preserved and the offset is appended. The resulting output `2026-05-01T09:00:00-05:00` is correct. BUT: if the client ever passes an already-offset string (e.g. `2026-05-01T09:00:00-05:00` from a previous round-trip), toISOWithTimezone would strip the trailing offset (line 41 only strips 'Z', not +HH:MM) and re-append a potentially different offset based on treating the offset portion as part of the naive datetime, producing a wrong result. This is latent but not currently triggered.
findings.impact: Scheduled posts could land at the wrong time if a pre-zoned datetime is round-tripped through updateDistribution.
findings.recommendation: Guard toISOWithTimezone to detect and skip strings that already have an offset (e.g. contain +/- after the time component), or validate input format before applying the transform.

findings.title: getCachedTaxonomyTerms ignores taxonomy argument in cache key — all taxonomies share the same stale cache entry
findings.description: unstable_cache is called with a fixed key array `['wp-taxonomy']` and the wrapped function takes `(taxonomy: string)` as a parameter. Next.js unstable_cache keys on (keyParts + serialized args) — the taxonomy argument IS included in the cache key automatically. So different taxonomy values DO produce different cache entries. This is NOT a bug in Next.js 15+. However the tag `['wp-taxonomies']` is the same for every taxonomy, so revalidateTag('wp-taxonomies') correctly invalidates all of them. This is safe. No finding.
findings.impact: None — this was investigated and found correct.

findings.title: WordPress publish: scheduledAt is passed for future posts but processor reads it from updatedMetadata, not original metadata — if transcription update overwrites metadata, scheduledAt survives only if the spread order is correct
findings.description: At line 207, the processor updates the job's metadata spreading `...currentMetadata` before adding transcript fields. currentMetadata is re-fetched from DB at that moment. The scheduledAt field written during job creation is in `job.metadata`, and `currentMetadata` is a fresh DB read — so scheduledAt is preserved. Then at line 237-241, `updatedMetadata` is fetched again after transcription. The WordPress publish at line 611 reads `updatedMetadata.scheduledAt`. This chain is correct IF no intermediate update drops scheduledAt from the spread. The risk is low given the spread pattern always includes the prior metadata.
findings.impact: Minimal — the chain appears correct but is fragile to any future intermediate metadata update that forgets to spread prior fields.
findings.recommendation: Consider moving scheduledAt and other critical scheduling fields to dedicated DB columns rather than opaque metadata JSON to eliminate the risk.

findings.title: verifyDistribution called with job.wpShowId but live-recording jobs mark the YouTube platform with externalId from the original YouTube URL — verifyYouTube uses job.wpShowId to get an OAuth token, which may be the wrong credentials for a video not uploaded via the portal
findings.description: For a live-recording job, the YouTube video was uploaded externally (not by the portal). verifyYouTube calls `getYouTubeAccessToken(wpShowId)` with the show's stored YouTube credentials. If those credentials do not have read access to the live-recorded video (e.g. it belongs to a different YouTube channel), the API returns 403 or an empty items array, and the verifier reports the video as 'not found'. This fires a false verification-failure notification for every live-recording episode.
findings.impact: Every live-recording distribution triggers a spurious 'video not found' verification failure email if the portal's YouTube credentials can't read the externally-owned video.
findings.recommendation: In verifyDistribution, skip the YouTube platform check when the job was a live recording (detectable via `job.metadata.existingYoutubeUrl` presence), or use a public (unauthenticated) YouTube Data API call for read-only verification.

findings.title: redirect() inside server action called after async DB operations — in Next.js App Router, redirect() throws a special error and must not be called inside a try/catch that catches all errors
findings.description: The server action `submitDistribution` calls `redirect('/login')` at lines 31 and 36. These redirects are at the top of the function before any try/catch, so they are safe here. No generic try/catch wraps the outer function body in this file, so redirect() can throw its special error unimpeded. This is correct. However, if a future developer adds a try/catch around the auth checks, redirect() calls inside would be swallowed. This is a latent risk, not a current bug.
findings.impact: No current bug. Latent risk if the function is wrapped in try/catch.
findings.recommendation: Document that redirect() must not be inside a try/catch in a code comment near the auth checks.

findings.title: yt-dlp cookies file written to tempDir but also cleaned up in the finally block before the upload completes — file is safe because upload happens before finally
findings.description: cookiesPath is written to tempDir at line 59. The yt-dlp execFileAsync call at line 81 uses the cookies file. The finally block at line 106 deletes all files in tempDir including the cookies file. Since yt-dlp reads the cookies file synchronously during its own execution and the await on execFileAsync completes before the finally runs, the cookies file is available for the entire yt-dlp invocation. This is safe. However: if yt-dlp forks a child process that outlives the await (it doesn't in normal operation), the cookies file would be gone. This is not a realistic concern.

findings.title: Manual path: if videoSource === 'upload' and the form is submitted (manual path), the uploadAndConfirmManual effect fires but videoFileRef may be null if the user switched video source tabs
findings.description: uploadAndConfirmManual checks `if (videoSource === 'upload')` and calls uploadVideoToGCS, which reads `videoFileRef.current`. When the user switches from 'upload' to 'youtube' and back, the 'youtube' toggle sets `videoFileRef.current = null` (line 678). If the user then picks a new file and submits, videoFileRef.current is correctly set by the onChange handler. BUT: if the user is on 'upload' mode, picks a file, switches to 'youtube', enters a URL, then switches back to 'upload' without re-picking a file — videoFileName is still shown (state from before the switch was cleared at line 679 by `setVideoFileName(null)`), but actually setVideoFileName IS called at line 679 to null, so the form correctly resets. The videoSourceReady guard prevents submission without a file. This is safe.
findings.impact: None — file state is correctly cleared on source tab switch.

residual_risks.risk: The 10-second delay before verifyDistribution (processor.ts:721) is inside a Promise that will keep the processJob promise alive for an additional 10 seconds after the job is marked complete. Combined with the 30-minute JOB_TIMEOUT_MS, this is fine in practice, but if the verification itself takes a long time (e.g., slow YouTube API), the total wall time could approach or exceed the timeout. The verification is in a try/catch so it won't fail the job, but it eats into the available timeout budget.

The brand taxonomy assignment `wpShowId === 21 ? 2 : 3` is a hard-coded magic number with no fallback if new shows are added to neither network, or if YDC's WordPress show ID changes. A mislabeled brand term would silently misfiled all future posts.

The yt-dlp binary is downloaded from GitHub at image build time with `latest` — no version pin. A breaking yt-dlp release would silently break all live-recording jobs at the next deploy without any code change.

testing_gaps: No test covers the retry path of a live-recording job where gcsPath is already populated (C-003 scenario).
No test verifies that verifyDistribution correctly handles a live-recording job's YouTube platform check (C-007 scenario).
No test exercises the title comparison in verifyYouTube with special characters (apostrophes, ampersands) to confirm no spurious failures.
No test covers the concurrent push race in verifyDistribution to confirm issue ordering is deterministic.
No test covers the updateDistribution scheduledAt path when status changes from 'future' to 'now' mid-flow (scheduleMode mismatch scenarios).
